speaker: interviewer
text: It's March 29, 1996. Interview with Fred Roer. What was it like for you when you had to leave your mother and brother? Can you describe that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that last goodbye?

speaker: interviewee
text: This notice came just within hours, we had to leave. Just an hour or so. You had time to just pack your clothes, and-- and you were brought back to the same railway station, put in-- in trains. Till-- but that time, it was already a-- a a cattle wagons. And you-- you really didn't have time to-- to say goodbye or what because everything was in such a uproar. Everything was still-- still new. The whole situation was still new. After 10 days, you still didn't know what was going on in the ghetto. And-- and this transport-- that transport I went in were 280 of-- of the just-arrived European Jews, what came from-- mostly from Germany. There were some from Vienna, some from Prague. The other ones were from Berlin, Frankfurt, Munich-- from cities in Germany. And we all were thrown together. A few of the boys knew each other. And we were then put in that-- in-- into-- to the cattle wagon and send away without knowing, again, where we were going. And we were told--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you, in the period when you were in Lodz, what was your-- what were the conversations with your mother? What-- how was she-- was she giving any explanation? Or were you comforting her and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody was comforting everybody else. Everybody was scouting around to get a feel of the-- of the situation. Was there a black market? Could you do something to get additional food? How were the other people living? What was going on? The-- the connection between the old inmates or people living their longer with the new one was kind of a friction because people-- you were afraid that everybody was taking advantage of you, which actually was because people were desperate. All-- everybody was in-- in a state of-- most of them were skeletons, the people what lived there before because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did anyone take advantage of you? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of me, personally? I don't think so. And we were a little careful-- my brother, and my mother, and myself-- because we wanted to get the feel of the place. And again, we-- we just scouted around what-- what was going on and how we could find the other place to live instead of with 30 people in one room, and-- which-- which my-- my brother and my mother did after a little while. After I was gone, I found out. In the beginning, when I was in Posen, I could receive pre-printed cards from my-- from-- from the ghetto into-- into the camp, just for a few months. And then that all broke down. But there, again, there were some hints what was going on when I noticed the change of address and so forth and so on. So I-- I got a hint what-- what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a hint?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they were establishing some kind of a living condition. Now, I was-- when we-- when the train arrived, we were with 280, like I said, European for-- of this newcomers into the ghetto. And all of a sudden, we were in Posen-- or this Poznanski, I think, in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- where we were put into a-- to a camp, which was-- which used to be a factory. And one room of that factory was emptied. There were along the walls were two tiers of bombs-- continuous tiers. And in the middle was a big bellies-- belly button stove-- or how do you call them-- them briquette ovens to heat the place? And then we were told we had to-- we would go out with the other people who were already that camp for months and months and circled with-- with barbed wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not-- it was not under the SS. The guards were not SS. The guards were Germans, which were direct under the Gestapo. The one who ran the whole deal was a big Gestapo officer. And we were in there. And then we-- the first day, they let us stay in the camp. The camp was run like the ghetto. There was a police chief. And there was a chief of the camp, which were Jews on the Jewish police. But outside was the-- the-- the Nazi guards, the-- not Nazi guards, the Nazi commanders and his-- his cronies. The camp was run by them and this so-called Jewish police. Some of them were real bad. They all had bull whips and would hit you and would take advantage of you in any way they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody was hit, more or less. This was-- but to-- to show us where we really were, I give you the drill, which is really gruesome. We-- the first Sunday, we were told-- Sunday, we didn't work. First Sunday, we were told to dress up in-- in good clothes. And then we were marched into the stadium of Posen. The stadium and the-- and the dressing rooms and underneath the stands was also a camp, which were working at the same-- well, I'll come back to what we were doing there. But to show us in which situation we were, we were marched into the stadium. The infield of the stadium, on a-- on the track, on one side in-- in front of the-- of the grandstands, there were five gallows with four noose each, with, like, a cross over. And on each end was a-- was a-- a noose. So with other words, there was room to hang 20 people. And then all of a sudden, they would bring out one at a time and hang them there. And the reason for being hanged was one had picked up a cigarette butt, stepped out of-- of formation. The other one had asked for a cigarette. One was caught trying to buy some bread. One was caught for minimal things like that, all along. That was reason to be hanged.

speaker: interviewee
text: So with other words, for any misstep in the-- in this camp, the-- the only penalty was death by hanging. So they hanged 20 people in front of us there. And then we had to march around the-- the people and looked up at them. So that was the introduction we got to that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about, I would say, about three week after we'd left Cologne. And I had never seen a dead person in my life before. So you can imagine what impact that had on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you thinking at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't whether you were thinking at all. You just were in a-- in a-- in a-- in a trance to go along with everything, what was happening to you. You didn't-- you really didn't realize what was going on for the first few weeks or months before you really got a hold of what was-- what was doing. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was the introduction we got there. And then we always had-- what we did in this camp was we had to build a lake. The Germans-- Posen, before World War I, was German. So the Germans wanted to make it a show city. And he wanted to build a man-made lake in there. And to make that lake, he had the Jews working on that-- all of different camps all around there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a tremendous, big lake. But I would say, if you put the blood from the people what were killed there into the lake, it would fill it. The-- the-- the overseer was a German. He had always his walking stick and would hit people like crazy. People were weak because the food was hardly any-- well, non-existent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: You would-- you would get-- in the morning, you would get a cup of warm water, which you would call coffee. For lunch, you would get the same soup you would get the night before, which was water with some vegetables boiled in-- boiled through. And every-- every second or third time, they would-- day, they would give you about two or three slices of bread and-- and a spoon of jam. And that would be the rations what they expected you to live on. The soup-- in the winter time, it gets real cold up there, even colder than Alaska because everything is flat. The wind-- windchill factor is just tremendous there. The vegetable would be dumped outside, and they all would be frozen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the soup would be boiled from the frozen beets-- maybe sugar beets-- or sometimes potatoes, or sometimes some kind of a melon. That would be the soup that they made it from. Naturally, you couldn't live on that. So what the good part was in that camp, we had our-- our own clothing, you-- our civilian clothing still. So we were working in them lakes, we would get in contact with the-- with Polish workers. And the Polish rations were very bad, too. But they had more to eat. They had victory gardens and stuff. But they didn't have any clothing. So you-- we would sell a shirt or pants or something for-- for additional food-- for sugar, for-- which, naturally, was one of the things if you were caught, you would be hanged. And-- Did you change-- exchange clothing for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was the only way you could survive there. Otherwise, you would die. The-- you-- you were full of lice. You were full of bed bugs. The-- them bunks-- there was straw laying on, about half a foot thick. This was just crawling with-- with-- with-- with insects. Your entertainment at night would be sitting there to crack the lice in your undershirt. But then I mentioned before that potbelly stove there. We would use that to cook extra food. Somebody would watch on the door, whether some of the guards would come into the camp at night. And then we would-- there, we would cook potatoes or whatever we had. So that was the additional food we would get. And one thing was in this-- all the camps, there was hardly anybody would steal anything, even when you were hungry because everybody was in the same situation. What was bad in the camp is some people, especially [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] high-- educated intellectual, they would kill themselves right away. They would hang themselves. They couldn't take it. And a lot of them would get sick. And when you'd get sick there-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say over 90% you wouldn't see anymore. They would be brought into a so-called hospital. And that hospital was just people to die. The ones what could not walk would be loaded into so-called ambulances. God darn-it. I'm talking too much. [DRINKS] The ambulance itself was with a big red cross, a [? white ?] [? deal. ?] And the people would be laid in there about 10 to 12 people. And then you would close the door. And at the same time, they would hook the-- the hose from the exhaust right to-- into a special connection to that box, that red cross box. Would drive around for an hour, and then people all would be dead. So this was one way of taking care of the-- of the sick people. We-- all we-- everybody had diarrhea, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Everybody got sick. But you-- you were afraid to stay home because they would bring you to the hospital. So you went to work, sick or not sick. And we had some doctors there. They told us-- what we did, for instance, for diarrhea-- the medication we-- we had, we would take wood and would burn it to ashes. And the ashes itselves would be-- would make-- we would take the-- eat the ashes as a-- as a medication.

speaker: interviewee
text: So supposedly, the-- the medication for-- for diarrhea is part of-- of ashes or something. We didn't know. But we had some doctors there what told us to do that. And it usually helped, to a certain extent.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, this camp, like I said before, at night, you had a big, like a [NON-ENGLISH] in your room where you could to release yourselves, to urinate only. The other one-- the-- the whole area was lit up. You had to walk to the latrine. And the latrine was quite to the side. But if you could avoid it, you wouldn't go because it was so cold during the nighttime. The washing facilities was one room, with around the wall was a trough, like a pig trough where they feed the pigs in. And above that, about a foot or so above it, there was a pipe-- a water pipe. Little holes were drilled in. And that was where the water came out. Now, in the wintertime, that would splash all over. And as high as the trough was, you would have the whole room one sheet of ice. So to-- to get the energy to wash yourselves was-- was something what took a lot of-- a lot of willpower because everybody was hungry. Everybody was feeling lousy. So everybody was dirty. Everybody was full of lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to keep yourselves clean was a big-- a big undertaking, especially in the wintertime. And if you didn't, you would even more would be more susceptible to the sicknesses of the camp. So what we did, we would take a rock and put steps into the ice to get into the washroom. So this was Posen. Later on, they gave up on the building that lake because the war machine was getting too close. And they they sending the Polish worker into Germany to work for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, we would be digging ditches for the sewer-- city sewer lines. And being a plumber, I was lucky, later on, to be able to work on laying the-- the sewer lines. And I was working with Polish workers then. And then there, again, I was able to get some extra food shoved to the side. And people started liking me. They saw I was-- I didn't know-- I was working with them. And this is one part, again, in Posen, that-- that I was able to-- to get extra food and stay alive, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Posen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Posen from, well, from '41-- from November '41 till the spring of '43. Then 80 of us were picked out and sent to the country. I don't know where we went there. We were shipped in the cattle wagon, again, to the country. Oh, one thing in Posen-- in the beginning, we were able to receive mail and also packages. Now, this Lagerfuhrer-- the leader, the lager, the camp-- this overseer from the-- the German, the Nazi-- he would-- allowed us get packages. Then when the packages were coming, all of a sudden, they would said, you can't have any more packages. He would steal it. He would just do the same with letters. He would tell us to put-- ask for money. And then he would confiscate the money. He would let it go for a week, then would steal it again. At that time, before we-- before we were ever evacuated, my brother was engaged. And they got married about three, four weeks before the evacuation started so that my brother and sister-in-law could go together. But the Nazis didn't allow it. And this way, my sister-in-law was still in Germany when I was in the camp. And I could write to her, like I explained before. And she would send me things. And there, we had a special code of the-- the bank director, for instance, his name was Joe-- Joseph. So I would try to tell Uncle Joseph to send me some-- something-- send me a bread. So she would understand to put some money into the bread. So she would send bread, and there was money inside or a cake or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was all things we-- we figured out, you know. But this-- also, this writing out to her was only for a few months. Then the whole thing stopped. So from Posen, we were sent to the country. And we were working on irrigating a river there for the-- for the farmers and so forth and so on. And-- and this camp, it was the best camp I was in.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sent with 80, and only one person got killed from spring of-- of '43 till-- till the fall of '43. Only he got killed because he-- he smuggled a letter out of there. The Gestapo intercepted it. And they came and hang him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the only thing because we were working out in the country. So we were working where the farmers had-- they would put their sugar beets, their corn, their potatoes in-- in storage for the winter. They would dig a hole out in the-- out in the-- on the farm. They would cover it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They would put into the hole-- a long, oh, about 10, 15 feet long-- they would put their potato or whatever they wanted to-- to save. Then you would cover it with straw. And over the straw, they would put more dirt. Now, when the guards weren't watching too close, we would crawl through the furrows and empty out them storage deals. So we would get extra food. And the guards were Polish people, which weren't too strict. They let us trap rabbits or birds. And whatever flew around or run around, we would trap it. And then we would cook it. And the only thing I would advise anybody is never to trap a crow. We-- we had a crow. We-- we boiled it for-- for two weeks. And nobody was able to eat it. It's the toughest meat you ever see. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that camp? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it near Posen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was near Posen. I would say, according to the train ride, I would say it was about 30, 40 miles away, not even 30, 40 kilometers-- about 20, 25 miles away. I don't even know what the rivers was or anything, what the name was. Like I said, and this-- this was the best camp we had. Then, all of a sudden, the camp-- we were loaded into-- one morning, instead of going to the river, we were-- take your things along. We going to go someplace else. So we were loaded into cattle wagon again. And after a little while, we were coupled onto a other train. No idea where we were going. And after-- what was it, a day and a night, I think-- right, we came to a place. Naturally, everything-- all you could-- all you could look out was a-- a-- a little window with-- with chicken wire in front of it, like a cattle wagon has. And we were 80 into this wagon-- pushed in, so it was quite crowded.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, the doors, naturally, were locked. All of a sudden, we heard a big commotion going on-- a band playing, people shouting, bumping things or hitting things against the walls of the train, just to make a big confusion-- more confusion than there was. And guns being shot off. What's going on? We didn't know what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, the door was opened. And some guy in SS uniform standing there-- out, out-- with a whip in his hand. And as you came out, you was-- was whipped already. And line up in rows of five. Everybody line up. And everybody was-- put your luggage in one pile. It will be brought after you into the-- into where you're going. And some guys in striped uniforms running around, some with whips in their hand. And some were just putting the-- the luggage all in one-- the one spot. And we still didn't know where we were. And there on the other side, there was a other rail tracks, where one train was unloaded for-- you could see people coming right out of Europe, still dressed in European-- still dressed in-- in Sunday dresses and good dresses. And the people started to being separated-- women and men, and children with the old people and the women. And it's a just a-- a man-made confusion. And then, we were march right into the camp. But the other ones were marched by somebody who said, to the right, to the left because we were-- we came all from a work camp already. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one-- one-- some of them guys in the uniforms-- well, you in Auschwitz. You in Auschwitz. What is Auschwitz? We didn't have any idea where we were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were-- there were incidents, which I never have talk about. So I don't think I want to-- what happened to people there when they were coming off the trains and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to just talk about what happened to you when you came off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The thing is too-- too crass, too cruel to even talk about it because today, I ask myself if it-- if it a dream or-- or it really happened because this was things-- not-- humans wouldn't do it. Only animals could be that cruel. So I think we skip that. I don't know whether it's worth talking about. Killing-- killing the people off-- just right off the bat for no reason at all. And-- and which style and which way they killed them, it's-- them guys all had guns with bayonets on them. And were pushing the people around.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were march right into the camp then, into Auschwitz, where we was told our luggage would come later on and to be sure everything is marked with our names. And then, when we got there, was in, I guess, around noon or afternoon-- I don't know-- we were told to undress and put the clothes in a neat pile so that we'll find it for later on when we get through with it. That was the-- the reception in Auschwitz.
